course_info.title: Mobile Communication Strategies
prerequisites: DC201 - Social Media Strategy and Management

learning_objectives: Analyze current mobile usage statistics and their implications for communications strategy
Explain mobile-first design principles and how they differ from desktop-first approaches
Identify mobile-specific communication channels: SMS, push notifications, in-app messaging, mobile web

lecture_notes.introduction: Over 60% of web traffic is mobile. Social media is overwhelmingly consumed on phones. Email is opened on mobile first. Any communications strategy that does not prioritize mobile is failing the majority of its audience.
key_concepts: Mobile dominance: smartphone penetration, screen time, and mobile commerce growth
Mobile-first design: designing for the smallest screen first and scaling up, not shrinking down
Mobile communication channels: SMS/MMS, push notifications, in-app messaging, mobile web, messaging apps
Micro-moments: intent-rich moments when people turn to their phone to learn, do, discover, or buy
Mobile user behavior: shorter sessions, vertical scrolling, thumb-zone optimization, distraction tolerance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Auditing a brand's mobile experience across all touchpoints: mobile website, app, email on mobile, social profiles on mobile, and messaging, identifying friction points and optimization opportunities

review.value: Google's framework for intent-driven mobile interactions: I-want-to-know, I-want-to-go, I-want-to-do, I-want-to-buy moments when people reflexively turn to their phone for immediate answers

The area of a mobile screen easily reachable by a one-handed thumb grip; key navigation and CTAs should be placed in the natural thumb zone, which varies by screen size but centers on the lower-middle portion of the screen

learning_objectives: Design compliant SMS marketing campaigns with proper opt-in and messaging cadence
Compare messaging platforms (WhatsApp, Facebook Messenger, iMessage) for business communication
Apply conversational marketing principles to messaging-based customer engagement
readings: TCPA and CTIA guidelines for SMS marketing

lecture_notes.introduction: SMS has a 98% open rate and 90% of texts are read within 3 minutes. Messaging apps are where people spend most of their mobile time. These channels offer unmatched immediacy but require careful strategy to avoid being perceived as intrusive.
key_concepts: SMS marketing compliance: TCPA requirements, express written consent, opt-out mechanisms
SMS content strategy: transactional messages, promotional messages, conversational messaging
Messaging app business features: WhatsApp Business, Messenger for Business, Apple Business Chat
Conversational marketing: using real-time messaging for customer service, sales, and engagement
Chatbot fundamentals: automated conversational interfaces for common queries and lead qualification
lecture_notes.demonstration: Setting up an SMS welcome sequence and promotional campaign in a platform like Twilio or SimpleTexting, including opt-in flow, welcome message, and promotional cadence with compliance safeguards
setup_requirements: SMS marketing platform trial (SimpleTexting or similar)
Messaging app business accounts for observation

review.value: The Telephone Consumer Protection Act requires express written consent before sending marketing texts, a clear opt-out mechanism in every message (reply STOP), and compliance with time-of-day restrictions; violations carry penalties of $500-$1,500 per message
Using real-time messaging interactions to move prospects through the funnel at their pace, replacing static forms and delayed email responses with immediate, personalized dialogue

learning_objectives: Design push notification strategies that drive engagement without causing opt-outs
Create in-app messaging campaigns for user onboarding, feature adoption, and retention
Apply behavioral triggers and segmentation to personalize mobile messaging

lecture_notes.introduction: Push notifications are the most powerful and most abused mobile communication channel. Done right, they deliver timely, relevant value. Done wrong, they get your app deleted. Understanding the psychology and strategy of push notifications separates effective mobile communicators from spammers.
key_concepts: Push notification permission: the critical opt-in moment and strategies to maximize acceptance rates
Push content strategy: transactional, informational, promotional, and re-engagement categories
Frequency optimization: finding the sending frequency that maximizes engagement without causing fatigue
Rich push notifications: images, action buttons, and interactive elements that increase engagement
In-app messaging: contextual messages displayed within the app triggered by user behavior or session context
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a push notification strategy for a fitness app: permission priming screen, onboarding sequence, workout reminders, achievement celebrations, and re-engagement for inactive users
setup_requirements: Mobile app wireframing tool (Figma or Sketch)

review.value: Showing a custom in-app screen explaining the value of push notifications before triggering the system permission dialog; primed users accept push permissions at 2-3x the rate of unprimed users because they understand the value exchange

Push notifications that open a specific screen within the app rather than the home screen, reducing friction between notification tap and desired action; critical for conversion-focused push campaigns

learning_objectives: Optimize web content, email, and social media for mobile consumption
Apply mobile-specific UX principles to communications design
Test and validate mobile experiences across devices and operating systems
readings: Google, 'Mobile-Friendly Test' documentation and PageSpeed Insights

lecture_notes.introduction: Optimizing for mobile is not about making desktop content smaller. It is about designing communications for the constraints and opportunities of mobile: smaller screens, touch interaction, intermittent attention, and location awareness.
key_concepts: Mobile-first content hierarchy: most important information first, progressive disclosure for details
Touch-friendly design: minimum 44x44px tap targets, adequate spacing, swipe-friendly interfaces
Mobile page speed: 53% of mobile users abandon sites that take more than 3 seconds to load
Responsive email design: single-column layouts, scalable images, mobile-friendly button sizing
Mobile testing: device labs, emulators, real-device testing protocols
lecture_notes.demonstration: Optimizing an existing landing page for mobile: restructuring content hierarchy, resizing touch targets, compressing images, and testing across iOS and Android using BrowserStack
setup_requirements: BrowserStack or similar cross-device testing tool

review.value: A mobile design pattern that shows essential information first and provides access to additional detail through expand/collapse, tabs, or scroll, reducing cognitive overload on small screens
Google research showing that 53% of mobile visitors abandon pages that take longer than 3 seconds to load; mobile page speed is both a UX necessity and an SEO ranking factor

learning_objectives: Track and analyze mobile user behavior using app analytics and mobile web analytics
Apply mobile attribution models to connect marketing activity to app installs and in-app actions
Build a mobile communications measurement framework with channel-specific KPIs

lecture_notes.introduction: Mobile measurement presents unique challenges: cross-device journeys, app store attribution gaps, and privacy restrictions on device-level tracking. Understanding mobile-specific analytics and attribution is essential for optimizing mobile communication strategies.
key_concepts: App analytics platforms: Firebase Analytics, Mixpanel, Amplitude for in-app behavior tracking
Mobile attribution: connecting marketing campaigns to app installs and in-app conversions
Cross-device attribution: tracking users who discover on mobile but convert on desktop, or vice versa
Privacy impact: iOS App Tracking Transparency, Google Privacy Sandbox, and their effect on attribution
Mobile KPIs: install rate, activation rate, retention (Day 1/7/30), session length, push opt-in rate
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a mobile communications dashboard combining app analytics (Firebase demo), mobile web analytics (GA4 mobile segment), and push notification performance to create a unified mobile view
setup_requirements: Firebase Analytics demo or sample data

review.value: Apple's iOS 14.5+ feature requiring user permission before apps can track activity across other apps and websites; opt-in rates average 25-35%, significantly reducing available attribution data for mobile campaigns
The percentage of app users who return on Day 1, Day 7, and Day 30 after install; the shape of this curve reveals whether the app delivers lasting value or is abandoned after initial curiosity

questions.question: Explain Google's micro-moments framework and describe how a retail brand would create content for each of the four micro-moment types.
questions.answer: Micro-moments are intent-driven mobile interactions: (1) I-want-to-know: a customer researches product reviews and comparisons. The brand creates helpful buying guides and comparison content optimized for mobile search. (2) I-want-to-go: a customer searches for nearby store locations. The brand optimizes Google Business Profile with hours, inventory, and directions. (3) I-want-to-do: a customer looks up how to use a product. The brand creates short tutorial videos and step-by-step guides. (4) I-want-to-buy: a customer is ready to purchase. The brand ensures a frictionless mobile checkout with saved payment options and one-tap purchasing.

rubric.key: Provides relevant retail content for each

questions.question: What is TCPA compliance for SMS marketing, and what are the consequences of sending marketing texts without proper consent?
questions.answer: The TCPA (Telephone Consumer Protection Act) requires express written consent before sending marketing text messages to consumers. Compliance includes: obtaining clear opt-in, providing opt-out in every message (STOP keyword), respecting time restrictions, and maintaining consent records. Consequences of non-compliance include penalties of $500 per unsolicited message and up to $1,500 per willful violation. These penalties are per-message, meaning a single campaign to 10,000 unconsented recipients could result in $5-15 million in fines. Class-action lawsuits under TCPA have resulted in settlements exceeding $100 million.

rubric.key: Notes per-message liability and scale risk

questions.question: What is permission priming for push notifications, and why does it significantly increase opt-in rates?
questions.answer: Permission priming is showing a custom in-app screen that explains the value of push notifications before triggering the system-level permission dialog. It increases opt-in rates (from ~40% to ~70%) because: (1) Users understand what they will receive and why it benefits them before making the decision. (2) The custom screen can be designed with compelling copy and visuals, unlike the generic system dialog. (3) If the user declines the priming screen, you can ask again later; but if they decline the system dialog, you cannot re-prompt without them manually changing settings. Priming prevents wasted permission requests.

rubric.key: Explains why it increases opt-in rates

questions.question: Explain the 3-second rule for mobile page speed and describe two techniques to improve mobile loading time.
questions.answer: The 3-second rule comes from Google research showing that 53% of mobile visitors abandon a page that takes more than 3 seconds to load, making speed critical for both user experience and SEO rankings. Two techniques: (1) Image optimization: compress images using modern formats (WebP), serve appropriately sized images for the device screen, and implement lazy loading so below-the-fold images load only when the user scrolls to them. (2) Critical CSS inlining: embedding the CSS needed for above-the-fold content directly in the HTML so it renders immediately, while loading the rest of the stylesheet asynchronously, reducing render-blocking resources.

rubric.key: States the 3-second rule with source data
First technique is specific and effective
Second technique is specific and effective

major_assignments.title: Mobile Communication Strategy
major_assignments.description: Develop a comprehensive mobile communication strategy for a brand, covering mobile web optimization, SMS/messaging, push notifications, and mobile content. Include a mobile audit of the current experience, strategic recommendations, and a measurement framework.
objectives: Audit a brand's current mobile experience across all touchpoints
Design mobile-specific strategies for SMS, push, and in-app channels
Optimize content and design for mobile consumption
Build a measurement framework with mobile-specific KPIs
deliverables: Mobile audit report covering website, email, social, and app experiences on mobile
Mobile communication strategy (8-10 pages) with channel-specific plans
Push notification and SMS campaign designs with content, triggers, and compliance
Mobile-optimized content examples (landing page, email, social post)
Measurement framework with mobile KPIs and attribution approach

criteria: Audit covers all mobile touchpoints systematically
Issues are specific and documented with screenshots
Competitor mobile experiences provide strategic context
Priorities are ranked by user impact
Strategy addresses all mobile channels appropriately
Recommendations are specific and actionable
Compliance requirements are addressed for SMS and push
Strategy aligns with broader business objectives
Mobile content examples demonstrate mobile-first design
Touch targets, typography, and load speed are optimized
Content hierarchy follows progressive disclosure principles
Accessibility on mobile is addressed
KPIs are mobile-specific and measurable
Attribution approach accounts for mobile challenges
Reporting plan connects mobile activity to business outcomes
Privacy considerations are addressed
Thorough mobile audit identifying genuine issues and opportunities
Strategic mobile communication plan across all channels
Mobile-optimized content demonstrating design and UX skills
Comprehensive measurement framework with privacy-aware attribution

quizzes.description: Regular quizzes on mobile strategy and channel-specific knowledge

weekly_exercises.description: Weekly mobile audit, design, and optimization exercises

midterm_examination.description: Practical examination on mobile optimization and channel strategy

final_examination.description: Cumulative examination including live mobile audit and strategy development

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all sessions. Mobile testing labs require in-person participation. More than 3 absences result in grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. Strategy documents submitted more than 5 days late will not be accepted.
course_policies.collaboration: Mobile testing exercises may involve partner work. All graded deliverables are individual. Sharing strategy documents between students is prohibited.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All audit data must be authentic. Screenshots and testing results must be unaltered. Mobile analytics data must come from stated sources.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need a smartphone (iOS or Android), laptop with Chrome DevTools, and access to SMS marketing and push notification platforms (free trials). BrowserStack educational access is provided.
